section.title: Lead Me in Your Righteousness
section.sectionRationale: A morning prayer for guidance, built on the contrast between a God who cannot dwell with evil and the abundant loving devotion that opens His house to those who take refuge in Him.
section.thesis: Because God delights in righteousness and has no pleasure in wickedness, David lays his morning plea before God's holy temple by His abundant loving devotion, asking to be led safely past his enemies while all who take refuge in the LORD rejoice under His favor.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the morning plea (vv1-3), God's holiness contrasted with the wicked (vv4-6), David's own approach by God's loving devotion (vv7-8), the deceit of the enemies (vv9-10), and the joy of those who take refuge in the LORD (vv11-12).

units.label: A morning plea
units.note: at daybreak I lay my plea before You

verses.text: Give ear to my words, O LORD; consider my groaning.
Attend to the sound of my cry, my King and my God, for to You I pray.
In the morning, O LORD, You hear my voice; at daybreak I lay my plea before You and wait in expectation.

units.label: God's holiness and the wicked
units.note: no evil can dwell with You

verses.text: For You are not a God who delights in wickedness; no evil can dwell with You.
The boastful cannot stand in Your presence; You hate all workers of iniquity.
You destroy those who tell lies; the LORD abhors the man of bloodshed and deceit.

units.label: Lead me by Your loving devotion
units.note: make straight Your way before me

verses.text: But I will enter Your house by the abundance of Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}; in reverence I will bow down toward Your holy temple.
Lead me, O LORD, in Your righteousness because of my enemies; make straight Your way before me.
For not a word they speak can be trusted; destruction lies within them. Their throats are open graves; their tongues practice deceit.
Declare them guilty, O God; let them fall by their own devices. Drive them out for their many transgressions, for they have rebelled against You.

units.label: Joy for those who take refuge

verses.text: But let all who take refuge in You rejoice; let them ever shout for joy. May You shelter them, that those who love Your name may rejoice in You.
For surely You, O LORD, bless the righteous; You surround them with the shield of Your favor.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, loyal covenant devotion, God's committed kindness to His people. David's confidence to enter God's house at all rests entirely on the abundance of this loving devotion, not on his own worthiness, a theme that runs through the whole Psalter.

basic.context: Psalm 5 is a morning prayer for guidance, set against enemies whose words cannot be trusted. David appeals first to God's own holy character, a God who cannot delight in wickedness, and then to God's abundant loving devotion as the only ground on which he can approach the holy temple at all.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm is prayed by a righteous sufferer surrounded by liars and slanderers, and it teaches that approaching God is never a matter of personal merit but always of His abundant loving devotion; the call for God to declare the wicked guilty is an appeal to divine justice, not private revenge.
basic.originalLanguages: David enters God's house only 'by the abundance of Your chesed,' steadfast covenant love, not by his own righteousness. The image of enemies whose throat is an open grave pictures speech that swallows the innocent, a vivid ancient Near Eastern figure for deadly deceit.
basic.typology: David's approach to God's house solely on the basis of God's loving devotion anticipates the gospel pattern completed in Christ, who is Himself the temple and the mercy by which sinners now enter God's presence, Hebrews 10:19-22.
basic.christ: Jesus is the one true righteous sufferer surrounded by deceitful accusers whose words could not be trusted, Mark 14:56, and He is also the one through whom every believer now enters God's presence, not by their own merit but by His mediating loving devotion.
basic.god: God is both utterly holy, unable to delight in wickedness or let evil dwell with Him, and abundantly loving, opening His house to sinners who come by His mercy rather than their own standing, a tension the whole psalm holds together without resolving it into either bare severity or soft sentiment.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:13 quotes this psalm's picture of throats as open graves to describe universal human sinfulness. Hebrews 10:19-22 describes entering God's presence by a new and living way opened in Christ. Psalm 91:4 echoes the image of God as a shield.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 5 teaches that no one enters God's presence by their own righteousness but only by His abundant loving devotion, a truth the whole Bible drives toward and fulfills in Jesus Christ, who opens the way into the true temple for all who take refuge in Him.

context.literary: A morning lament structured around a series of contrasts: God's holiness against the wicked's deceit, and David's own approach by mercy against the enemies' downfall by their own devices, closing in communal joy for those who take refuge in the LORD.
context.historical: The superscription, for the choirmaster with the flutes, situates this among psalms composed for temple worship, likely used in morning liturgy given its explicit references to daybreak prayer and entering God's house.
context.canonical: Part of a cluster of morning and evening psalms, alongside Psalms 3 and 4, that shape a daily rhythm of prayer built on trust in God's character rather than in circumstances.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model morning prayer that rests entirely on God's holy character and abundant mercy, teaching the worshipper both to expect nothing from personal merit and everything from God's loving devotion when approaching Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific situation surrounded by deceitful enemies; it prescribes for every worshipper the pattern of approaching God by His mercy alone and appealing to His justice against evil rather than taking justice into one's own hands.

debates.question: Is the request 'declare them guilty, O God, let them fall by their own devices' a call for personal vengeance?

views.label: An appeal to God's justice, not personal revenge
views.case: David asks God, the righteous Judge, to act, and pictures the wicked falling into traps of their own making, language of just consequence rather than personal cruelty inflicted by David's own hand.

views.label: An imprecation to be read carefully in light of Christ's teaching to love enemies
views.case: Jesus commands prayer for persecutors, Matthew 5:44, so such prayers should be understood as longing for God's justice and the defeat of evil, offered alongside genuine hope for the enemy's repentance, rather than simple wishes for their ruin.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm David is appealing to God's justice rather than acting as his own avenger, a pattern the New Testament commends, Romans 12:19.

study.originalLanguages: David enters God's house only berov chasdecha, by the abundance of Your steadfast love, chesed, grounding his access entirely in God's covenant devotion rather than personal merit. The wicked's throat is described as an open grave, a vivid image of speech that consumes and destroys, quoted directly by Paul in Romans 3:13 to describe universal sin.

typology.type: Entering God's house by His abundant loving devotion alone, Psalm 5:7
typology.fulfillment: Believers entering God's presence through Christ's mediating mercy, not their own righteousness, Hebrews 10:19-22.

study.christ: Jesus is the righteous sufferer surrounded by false witnesses whose testimony could not agree, Mark 14:56-59, embodying David's complaint that not a word his enemies speak can be trusted. Yet Jesus is also greater than David's temple hope: He is Himself the new and living way by which every believer, having no righteousness of their own, now enters God's presence, exactly as David could only enter by God's abundant loving devotion.
study.god: God is holy in a way that cannot coexist with evil, no evil can dwell with Him, and yet abundantly merciful, opening His house to those who come by His loving devotion rather than their own standing. This same God who cannot delight in wickedness delights to shield and shelter all who take refuge in Him.

crossReferences.note: Quotes this psalm's image of an open-grave throat to describe the universal deceitfulness of human sin.
Describes believers entering God's presence by a new and living way opened through Christ, echoing David's entry by mercy alone.
Echoes the image of God as a shield surrounding those who take refuge in Him.

study.oneStory: Psalm 5 teaches that access to God has never rested on human merit but always on His abundant loving devotion, a pattern fulfilled completely in Jesus Christ, the righteous sufferer who opens the true temple to every sinner who takes refuge in Him.

quiz.q: What does David ask God to do at the start of Psalm 5?

options.text: Give ear to his words, consider his groaning, and attend to his cry, since he lays his plea before God every morning.
options.note: This is the psalm's opening morning petition.

options.text: Reveal the identity of his enemies to him.
options.note: David is aware of deceitful enemies already; his opening plea is simply for God to hear him, not to reveal identities.

options.text: Grant him victory in an upcoming battle.
options.note: The psalm's opening concern is being heard in prayer, not a specific battle.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm opens in silence.
options.note: Verses 1-3 are an urgent, explicit plea to be heard.

quiz.q: What does Psalm 5:4-6 reveal about God's character?

options.text: He does not delight in wickedness, and no evil, boasting, or deceit can dwell in His presence.
options.note: This section describes God's holiness in relation to evil directly and repeatedly.

options.text: He is indifferent to human sin.
options.note: The passage shows the opposite, God actively opposed to evil, not indifferent.

options.text: He delights equally in good and evil.
options.note: The text explicitly states God does not delight in wickedness; there is no equal delight in evil.

options.text: He has no moral character at all.
options.note: This section is precisely about God's clear, holy moral character.

quiz.q: On what basis does David say he will enter God's house in verse 7?

options.text: By the abundance of God's loving devotion, not his own merit.
options.note: This is the psalm's central statement about access to God's presence.

options.text: By his own perfect righteousness.
options.note: David explicitly credits access to God's mercy, chesed, not his own righteousness.

options.text: By offering a large sacrifice.
options.note: The verse specifically names God's abundant loving devotion as the basis, not a specific sacrifice.

options.text: By his royal status as king.
options.note: The verse gives no basis in royal status; it credits God's mercy alone.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the speech of the enemies in verse 9?

options.text: Not a word can be trusted; their throats are open graves and their tongues practice deceit.
options.note: Paul quotes this very image in Romans 3:13 to describe universal human sinfulness.

options.text: They speak truthfully but act wickedly.
options.note: The verse specifically targets their speech itself as untrustworthy, not merely a mismatch between words and actions.

options.text: They rarely speak at all.
options.note: The verse describes active, deceitful speech, not silence.

options.text: Their words are mostly harmless exaggeration.
options.note: The image of an open grave describes deadly, destructive speech, far beyond harmless exaggeration.

quiz.q: What is promised to those who take refuge in the LORD in verses 11-12?

options.text: Joy, shelter, and God's favor surrounding them like a shield.
options.note: The psalm closes with this triple promise for those who trust in God.

options.text: Freedom from all future hardship.
options.note: The psalm promises God's favor and shelter, not a guarantee against all future hardship.

options.text: Wealth and social status.
options.note: The promise is joy and divine shelter, not material wealth or status.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm ends without any promise.
options.note: Verses 11-12 explicitly promise joy, shelter, and God's favor.

questions: David brings his morning prayer to God before facing the day's enemies and challenges. What would it look like for you to lay your plea before God at the start of your day rather than as an afterthought?

David is confident he can only enter God's house by the abundance of God's loving devotion, not his own merit. Where are you tempted to think your standing with God depends on your own performance rather than His mercy?

The psalm names the specific danger of deceitful speech, words that destroy like an open grave. Where might careless or deceptive words, yours or others', be doing quiet damage in your relationships right now?
David asks God to deal justly with his enemies rather than taking revenge himself. Is there a situation where you need to release your desire for personal vengeance and trust God's justice instead?

The psalm ends in joy for those who take refuge in the LORD, sheltered like something surrounded by a shield. Where do you need to actively take refuge in God this week rather than trying to shield yourself?

translationNotes.note: Lay my plea before You can also be rendered order my prayer, using an image of arranging a sacrifice on an altar, suggesting careful, deliberate morning prayer rather than a hurried request.
By the abundance of Your loving devotion translates berov chasdecha, grounding all access to God's house in His covenant chesed rather than any human merit or ritual qualification.
Their throats are open graves is quoted by Paul in Romans 3:13 as part of his composite Old Testament indictment of universal human sinfulness in Romans 3:10-18.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 5:1-12. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster could be confirmed this session for Psalm 5, so sermon status is none.
